nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found in small groups on drop-offs from 30 to 60 m, near caves or ledges of steep outer reef slopes; depth range 7–70 m

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and ledges

diet.notes: Feeds on crustacean larvae and fish eggs in the wild. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and over time high-quality flake foods. A refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is generally considered beneficial to provide a steady supply of live zooplankton. Multiple small feedings per day are advisable given the species' high metabolism.

avoidWith: other anthias males in small systems

summary: A striking deep-water anthias from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered more docile than most anthias varieties; best kept as a harem (one male, multiple females) in a reef aquarium with ample hiding places and frequent feedings.
careNotes:
Pseudanthias lori is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual will transition to male over time. Like most anthias, it has been known to form harems with one male and multiple females; keeping a group of three or more (one male with two or more females) is considered more natural and may reduce stress on the single male.

LiveAquaria describes this species as more docile than most anthias, making it somewhat easier to keep in a community reef setting. Numerous caves and hiding places are generally advised, reflecting its natural habitat on deep, steep reef slopes.

Minimum tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 70 gallons (~265 L) for groups. Given the haremic grouping requirement and the species' active swimming behavior, 300 L is used here as the conservative recommendation from the playbook nice-values list. A single specimen may be housed in somewhat smaller systems (around 200 L), though group keeping is considered more appropriate.

Temperature source disagreement: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase lists a known range of 22–27°C and a preferred range of 25.3–29°C. The averaged range of 22–26°C is used here. Keeping the upper bound closer to 26–27°C may be appropriate for specimens displaying stress at lower temperatures.

Taxonomy note: FishBase currently recognizes this species as Pyronotanthias lori (Lubbock & Randall, 1976) following reclassification of the Anthiadinae into the family Anthiadidae. LiveAquaria and much of the aquarium trade continue to use the older placement Pseudanthias lori (Serranidae). Anthiadidae is used here as the more current authority; 'Pyronotanthias lori' is listed in alsoKnownAs.

Family disagreement: LiveAquaria lists family Serranidae; FishBase lists Anthiadidae. Anthiadidae is used here as the current accepted classification.

This species was described approximately 25 years before its trade availability and is sourced primarily from Cook Islands and Fiji. It has been reported to be harvested from relatively deep water (30–60 m), which may contribute to the need for acclimation care on arrival.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered difficult. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change occurs socially when the dominant male is absent. Specific captive breeding records for this species are not well documented in available sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm max), temperature range (22–27°C), native range (Indo-Pacific, Christmas Island to Tuamotu Islands, north to Japan, south to northern GBR), taxonomy (Anthiadidae / Perciformes), and habitat (reef drop-offs 30–60 m). FishBase uses the updated name Pyronotanthias lori; LiveAquaria uses Pseudanthias lori. FishBase also lists the preferred temp range as 25.3–29°C, higher than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); averaged upper bound used (26°C). Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; BlueZoo did not have a page for this species; Fishkeeping World general anthias page returned 404; TFH Magazine returned 404 — FishBase is the confirmed secondary.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change occurs socially. No well-documented captive breeding records available for this species specifically.
